title: Real Zaragoza

infobox.value: (Spanish pronunciation: [[reˈal θaɾaˈɣoθa]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Spanish "Help:IPA/Spanish")), commonly referred to as **Zaragoza**, is a football club based in [Zaragoza](/wiki/Zaragoza "Zaragoza"), [Aragon](/wiki/Aragon "Aragon"), Spain, that currently competes in the [Segunda División](/wiki/Segunda_Divisi%C3%B3n "Segunda División"), the second tier of the [Spanish league system](/wiki/Spanish_football_league_system "Spanish football league system"). Zaragoza holds its home games at [La Romareda](/wiki/La_Romareda "La Romareda").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

**Real Zaragoza, S.A.D.** (Spanish pronunciation: [[reˈal θaɾaˈɣoθa]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Spanish "Help:IPA/Spanish")), commonly referred to as **Zaragoza**, is a football club based in [Zaragoza](/wiki/Zaragoza "Zaragoza"), [Aragon](/wiki/Aragon "Aragon"), Spain, that currently competes in the [Segunda División](/wiki/Segunda_Divisi%C3%B3n "Segunda División"), the second tier of the [Spanish league system](/wiki/Spanish_football_league_system "Spanish football league system"). Zaragoza holds its home games at [La Romareda](/wiki/La_Romareda "La Romareda").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Founded on 18 March 1932, the club has spent the majority of its history in [La Liga](/wiki/La_Liga "La Liga"), although they have not played at that level since they were last relegated in 2013. They have won the [Copa del Rey](/wiki/Copa_del_Rey "Copa del Rey") six times, [1963–64 Inter-Cities Fairs Cup](/wiki/1963%E2%80%9364_Inter-Cities_Fairs_Cup "1963–64 Inter-Cities Fairs Cup") and the [1994–95 UEFA Cup Winners' Cup](/wiki/1994%E2%80%9395_UEFA_Cup_Winners%27_Cup "1994–95 UEFA Cup Winners' Cup"), amongst other trophies. Traditionally, their team colours are white shirts and socks with royal blue shorts.

A government survey in 2007 found that 2.7% of the Spanish population support Real Zaragoza, making them the seventh-most supported in the country.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

The club's main rivals are: [Huesca](/wiki/SD_Huesca "SD Huesca"), their opponents in the [Aragonese derby](/wiki/Aragonese_derby "Aragonese derby");[[4]](#cite_note-4) [Numancia](/wiki/CD_Numancia "CD Numancia"), from the nearby [Province of Soria](/wiki/Province_of_Soria "Province of Soria");[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) and [Osasuna](/wiki/CA_Osasuna "CA Osasuna"), the largest club in the neighbouring [Navarre](/wiki/Navarre "Navarre") region.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Real_Zaragoza&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Early years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Real_Zaragoza&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early years")]

Real Zaragoza was originally formed from two rival teams: *[Iberia SC](/wiki/Iberia_SC "Iberia SC")* and *[Real Zaragoza CD](/wiki/Zaragoza_CD "Zaragoza CD")*. In 1939, after three years without football due to the [Spanish Civil War](/wiki/Spanish_Civil_War "Spanish Civil War"), the team made its first appearance in [La Liga](/wiki/La_Liga "La Liga"), [ending in 7th position out of 12 teams](/wiki/1939%E2%80%9340_La_Liga "1939–40 La Liga"), but [being relegated in 1941](/wiki/1940%E2%80%9341_La_Liga "1940–41 La Liga"). The club returned to the top division one year later, only to be [immediately relegated back](/wiki/1942%E2%80%9343_La_Liga "1942–43 La Liga").[[9]](#cite_note-9) It remained in [Segunda División](/wiki/Segunda_Divisi%C3%B3n "Segunda División") until the end of the [1950–51 campaign](/wiki/1950%E2%80%9351_Segunda_Divisi%C3%B3n "1950–51 Segunda División"), when it achieved promotion by finishing second in a play-off league.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

On 8 September 1957, the team left its original stadium, El Torrero, for its current stadium, [La Romareda](/wiki/La_Romareda "La Romareda").

### The golden era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Real_Zaragoza&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: The golden era")]

Beginning in the [1960–61 season](/wiki/1960%E2%80%9361_La_Liga "1960–61 La Liga"), Zaragoza enjoyed a period of great success, showcasing some of the greatest players playing in Spain during that decade, which earned for themselves the designation of *Los Magníficos*. While the team failed to capture the league title, it finished in the top five every year until [1968–69](/wiki/1968%E2%80%9369_La_Liga "1968–69 La Liga"), with two third-place finishes, and also won its first two [Copa del Rey](/wiki/Copa_del_Rey "Copa del Rey") titles and the [1963–64 Inter-Cities Fairs Cup](/wiki/1963%E2%80%9364_Inter-Cities_Fairs_Cup "1963–64 Inter-Cities Fairs Cup").

Zaragoza's famous attacking line included [Canário](/wiki/Can%C3%A1rio "Canário"), [Carlos Lapetra](/wiki/Carlos_Lapetra "Carlos Lapetra"), [Marcelino](/wiki/Marcelino_Mart%C3%ADnez "Marcelino Martínez"), [Eleuterio Santos](/wiki/Eleuterio_Santos "Eleuterio Santos") and [Juan Manuel Villa](/wiki/Juan_Manuel_Villa "Juan Manuel Villa").[[11]](#cite_note-11) The Peruvian [Juan Seminario](/wiki/Juan_Seminario "Juan Seminario"), who started his career in Spain with *Los Maños* before moving to [Barcelona](/wiki/FC_Barcelona "FC Barcelona"), won the [Pichichi Trophy](/wiki/Pichichi_Trophy "Pichichi Trophy") in the [1961–62 campaign](/wiki/1961%E2%80%9362_La_Liga "1961–62 La Liga"), scoring 25 goals in 30 matches as Zaragoza finished in fourth position.

### 1970s to the end of the century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Real_Zaragoza&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1970s to the end of the century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/Real_Zaragoza_1994-1995.svg/250px-Real_Zaragoza_1994-1995.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Real_Zaragoza_1994-1995.svg)

The starting XI in the [1995 Cup Winners' Cup final](/wiki/1995_UEFA_Cup_Winners%27_Cup_Final "1995 UEFA Cup Winners' Cup Final")

Zaragoza finished third in [1973–74](/wiki/1973%E2%80%9374_La_Liga "1973–74 La Liga") and a best-ever second in [the following season](/wiki/1974%E2%80%9375_La_Liga "1974–75 La Liga"), losing the title in the last round to [Real Madrid](/wiki/Real_Madrid_C.F. "Real Madrid C.F."). The club was also defeated 0–1 in the [1976 domestic cup final](/wiki/1975%E2%80%9376_Copa_del_Rey "1975–76 Copa del Rey") against [Atlético Madrid](/wiki/Atl%C3%A9tico_Madrid "Atlético Madrid"),[[12]](#cite_note-12) spending two seasons in the second level during the decade, with promotion at the first attempt on either occasion.

In [1986](/wiki/1985%E2%80%9386_Copa_del_Rey "1985–86 Copa del Rey"), Zaragoza won its third Copa del Rey, defeating Barcelona 1–0. The club finished the [1990–91 season](/wiki/1990%E2%80%9391_La_Liga "1990–91 La Liga") in 17th position, thus having to appear in the [promotion/relegation](/wiki/Promotion_and_relegation "Promotion and relegation") play-offs against [Real Murcia](/wiki/Real_Murcia "Real Murcia"); on 19 June 1991, after a 0–0 away draw, a 5–2 home win meant the team managed to maintain its top level status.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Víctor Fernández](/wiki/V%C3%ADctor_Fern%C3%A1ndez "Víctor Fernández") was appointed manager in 1991. On 10 May 1995, [one year](/wiki/1994%E2%80%9395_Real_Zaragoza_season "1994–95 Real Zaragoza season") after [winning the Copa del Rey](/wiki/1993%E2%80%9394_Copa_del_Rey "1993–94 Copa del Rey") against [Celta](/wiki/Celta_de_Vigo "Celta de Vigo"), Zaragoza [won](/wiki/1994%E2%80%9395_UEFA_Cup_Winners%27_Cup "1994–95 UEFA Cup Winners' Cup") the [UEFA Cup Winners' Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Cup_Winners%27_Cup "UEFA Cup Winners' Cup") against [Arsenal](/wiki/Arsenal_F.C. "Arsenal F.C.") at the [Parc des Princes](/wiki/Parc_des_Princes "Parc des Princes"), [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris"), after having disposed of the likes of [Feyenoord](/wiki/Feyenoord "Feyenoord") and [Chelsea](/wiki/Chelsea_F.C. "Chelsea F.C.") *en route*. With the score level at 1–1, the two teams entered [extra time](/wiki/Overtime_(sports) "Overtime (sports)") and, in the 120th minute, [Nayim](/wiki/Nayim "Nayim") hit a half-volley from just past the halfway line, putting it beyond the reach of goalkeeper [David Seaman](/wiki/David_Seaman "David Seaman") for the final [2–1](/wiki/1995_UEFA_Cup_Winners%27_Cup_Final "1995 UEFA Cup Winners' Cup Final").[[13]](#cite_note-13) The club then contested the [1995 UEFA Super Cup](/wiki/1995_UEFA_Super_Cup "1995 UEFA Super Cup") against [Ajax](/wiki/AFC_Ajax "AFC Ajax"), losing 1–5 on aggregate despite a home draw in the first leg.[[14]](#cite_note-14) Víctor Fernández was dismissed from his post in early November 1996, after only winning one league match [that season](/wiki/1996%E2%80%9397_La_Liga "1996–97 La Liga").[[15]](#cite_note-15)

### The 21st century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Real_Zaragoza&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: The 21st century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/Celebracion_Real_Zaragoza_2013_postiga_%28cropped%29.JPG/250px-Celebracion_Real_Zaragoza_2013_postiga_%28cropped%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:Celebracion_Real_Zaragoza_2013_postiga_(cropped).JPG)

Players celebrate a goal by [Hélder Postiga](/wiki/H%C3%A9lder_Postiga "Hélder Postiga") during the [2012–13 season](/wiki/2012%E2%80%9313_La_Liga "2012–13 La Liga").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Real_Zaragoza_league_performance_1929-2023.svg/250px-Real_Zaragoza_league_performance_1929-2023.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Real_Zaragoza_league_performance_1929-2023.svg)

Chart of Real Zaragoza league performance 1929–2023

The 2000s brought a further two Copa del Rey titles to Zaragoza's trophy cabinet, including the [2003–04 edition](/wiki/2003%E2%80%9304_Copa_del_Rey "2003–04 Copa del Rey") against Real Madrid in [Barcelona](/wiki/Barcelona "Barcelona") (3–2 after extra time).[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17) However, the club also suffered top flight relegation in [2002](/wiki/2001%E2%80%9302_La_Liga "2001–02 La Liga")[[18]](#cite_note-18) after [narrowly avoiding so the previous season](/wiki/2000%E2%80%9301_La_Liga "2000–01 La Liga"),[[19]](#cite_note-19) but [achieved immediate promotion in 2003](/wiki/2002%E2%80%9303_Segunda_Divisi%C3%B3n "2002–03 Segunda División").[[20]](#cite_note-20) In late May 2006, Agapito Iglesias purchased Alfonso Solans' shares and took control of the club, promising to build one of the strongest teams in Spain and Europe. In his first year in charge, he purchased [Pablo Aimar](/wiki/Pablo_Aimar "Pablo Aimar") from [Valencia](/wiki/Valencia_CF "Valencia CF") for [€](/wiki/Euro "Euro")11 million,[[21]](#cite_note-21) and former manager Víctor Fernández also returned to the club.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

Mainly due to [Diego Milito](/wiki/Diego_Milito "Diego Milito")'s 23 goals in [2006–07](/wiki/2006%E2%80%9307_La_Liga "2006–07 La Liga") (he finished third to [Roma](/wiki/A.S._Roma "A.S. Roma")'s [Francesco Totti](/wiki/Francesco_Totti "Francesco Totti") and Real Madrid's [Ruud van Nistelrooy](/wiki/Ruud_van_Nistelrooy "Ruud van Nistelrooy") – 26 and 25 goals, respectively – in the [European Golden Shoe](/wiki/European_Golden_Shoe "European Golden Shoe") race), Real Zaragoza finished in sixth position, thus [qualifying](/wiki/2007%E2%80%9308_UEFA_Cup "2007–08 UEFA Cup") to the [UEFA Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Europa_League "UEFA Europa League"). However, [the following season](/wiki/2007%E2%80%9308_La_Liga "2007–08 La Liga") ended in relegation (18th position among 20 teams with only 10 wins in 38 matches, among them only 1 away win in 19 games)[[23]](#cite_note-23) – for the second time in the decade – with the side also being eliminated in the first round in [European competition](/wiki/UEFA "UEFA"). Legendary club coach Víctor Fernández returned for a second spell, although he was sacked in January 2008,[[24]](#cite_note-24) as the club had four managers during the campaign. On the last matchday, a brace from [Ricardo Oliveira](/wiki/Ricardo_Oliveira "Ricardo Oliveira") proved insufficient in a 2–3 away loss against [Mallorca](/wiki/RCD_Mallorca "RCD Mallorca"), with the team totalling 42 points to [Osasuna](/wiki/CA_Osasuna "CA Osasuna")'s 43.

Zaragoza achieved promotion from the second division [at the first attempt](/wiki/2008%E2%80%9309_Segunda_Divisi%C3%B3n "2008–09 Segunda División"). On the last matchday, on 20 June 2009, the team drew 2–2 at [Rayo Vallecano](/wiki/Rayo_Vallecano "Rayo Vallecano") with goals from youth graduate [David Generelo](/wiki/David_Generelo "David Generelo") and ex-Real Madrid [defender](/wiki/Defender_(association_football) "Defender (association football)") [Francisco Pavón](/wiki/Francisco_Pav%C3%B3n "Francisco Pavón"), only trailing champions [Xerez](/wiki/Xerez_CD "Xerez CD") in the table. Nevertheless, that season Zaragoza was the best team at home, gained 50 from their 81 points in home games.[[25]](#cite_note-25) However, after four seasons mainly spent in the bottom half of the table, Zaragoza was relegated following the [2012–13 Liga season](/wiki/2012%E2%80%9313_La_Liga "2012–13 La Liga") after finishing last.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

In April 2022, the purchase of 51% of the shares of the SAD by an international investment fund led by [Jorge Mas](/wiki/Jorge_Mas "Jorge Mas") was disclosed.[[27]](#cite_note-27)
